text: The surgeon approaches the abdomen through a midline laparotomy incision, revealing the previous side-to-side stapled anastomosis from the closure of the ileostomy.
The surgeon seals and divides the mesenteric vessels using a ligature, then staples and divides the terminal ileum just proximal to the ileocecal valve.
The surgeon measures fifteen centimeters from the transected terminal ileum to ensure adequate small bowel length for the formation of the nipple valve and continent ileostomy.
The surgeon inserts a suture to mark the top of the S pouch after measuring the first fifteen centimeters, with additional sutures marking each subsequent limb.
The surgeon positions the first and second limbs, as well as the second and third limbs of the small bowel adjacent to each other, joining them with a continuous 3-0 vicryl seromuscular suture.
The surgeon joins the second and third adjacent limbs of the small bowel using a continuous suture technique.

The surgeon uses diathermy to mark the line of incision along each limb on either side of the suture line, extending the marks along the full length of the joined loops of small bowel to maximize the pouch's capacity.
The surgeon carefully incises the small bowel along the previously marked incision lines, laying open the bowel to reveal the interior structures.
The surgeon sequentially opens each limb of the pouch, taking care to avoid damaging the posterior wall of the lumen during the process.
The surgeon carefully turns each corner of the small bowel pouch, ensuring that the sutures are not damaged during the maneuver.
Each loop of ileum is laid open, revealing the interior of the bowel segments as part of the pouch formation process.
The surgeon sutures the adjacent posterior walls of the small bowel together using a continuous three ovicle suture, employing a full thickness stitch to ensure secure closure.
The surgeon positions two adjacent limbs of the ileal pouch to form the posterior wall, ensuring proper alignment and avoiding damage to the lumen.
The surgeon sutures the second and third limbs together, using a continuous suture technique to join the posterior walls of the ileal loops.
The surgeon completes the posterior wall suturing of the ileum loops, ensuring the posterior walls are securely joined.

The surgeon forms the anterior wall of the pouch by suturing the lateral edges of the original first and third limbs of the S. Stay sutures are visible at either end of the pouch and in the middle of the anterior wall.
The surgeon uses a continuous suture to join the lower half of the pouch, completing the initial stage of pouch formation.
The top half of the pouch remains open, providing access for the formation of the nipple valve.
The surgeon uses diathermy to thin out the mesenteric fat bordering the terminal ileum, preparing the area for the interception required in the nipple valve formation.
The surgeon uses a diathermy scratch pad to rub the serosal surface of the terminal ileum, preparing the tissue for further surgical manipulation.
The loops of interseceptive bowel are positioned to promote adhesion between them, facilitating the formation of the nipple valve.
The surgeon enhances tissue granulation to prevent slippage of the interseceptive bowel loops, ensuring stability for the nipple valve formation.
The surgeon rubs the serosal surface of the terminal ileum with a diathermy scratch pad, continuing the process to promote adhesion.
The surgeon applies a technique similar to pleurodesis, causing visible irritation between the visceral layers to increase adhesion at the site of interception, aiding in nipple valve formation.
The surgeon carefully incises the peritoneum over the mesentery at the designated site for the nipple valve, followed by attempts to intercept the ileum.
The surgeon excises fat tissue adjacent to the small bowel, carefully removing it from the surrounding area.
The surgeon makes another attempt to intercept the terminal ileum, using careful traction to successfully form the interception.
A soft silastic drain is inserted through the nipple valve into the distal ileum, extending into the top of the pouch.
The surgeon sutures the rolled edge of the ileum using interrupted serosal sutures, securing the tissue with precise needle placement through a vial.
The surgeon uses a Reticulator-Fifty-Five stapler equipped with a green, 4.8 mm cartridge to staple the tissue.
The surgeon places a double staggered row of titanium staples along the tissue, securing the surgical site.
The surgeon uses a stapler to fire lactamir copolymer staples at three separate points around the inter-suscepted small bowel, with the staples being totally absorbable.
The surgeon closes the top of the anterior wall of the pouch around the nipple valve, completing the closure of this section.
The surgeon inserts a series of interrupted sutures at a potentially weak point in the surgical site to minimize the risk of leakage.
The surgeon places additional interrupted sutures to secure closure at the top of the pouch and along the anterior wall, ensuring a tight seal.
The nipple valve and inserted tube are visible within the pouch. Some anterior wall sutures are placed to anchor the nipple valve by picking up part of the inter-susceptive valve.
The reticulator staple is inserted within the pouch and into the end of the nipple valve, followed by the careful withdrawal of the drain.
The surgeon performs a final firing of the reticulator staple to secure the nipple valve to the anterior wall of the pouch, completing the formation of the anastomosis.

The surgeon tests the pouch for leaks by filling it with normal saline, observing for any fluid escape. The capacity of the pouch is assessed, and the patency of the nipple valve is verified through repeated catheterization.
The surgeon removes and reinserts the thylastic drain within the pouch, ensuring adequate drainage.
The surgeon inserts a second layer of interrupted sutures along the anterior anastomosis using Ethibond sutures, reinforcing the connection and securing the structure.
The surgeon makes a small skin incision, measuring 1.5 to 2 centimeters, in the lower right iliac fossa and extends it through the anterior abdominal wall.
The surgeon inserts a small Alexis wound protector to aid in the exteriorization of the terminal ileum, ensuring clear access to the surgical site.
The surgeon visually inspects the pouch to ensure it is not twisted and confirms that it is lying in the correct orientation within the abdominal cavity.
The surgeon uses the drain to confirm the patency of the nipple valve, fills the pouch with normal saline, and re-catheterizes the ileostomy to ensure proper drainage.
The surgeon removes the drain from the ileostomy to confirm its continence after the pouch is filled with normal saline.
The surgeon excises the excess ileum and forms the ileostomy, ensuring it is flush with the skin surface.
The surgeon secures the exteriorized terminal ileum using a series of interrupted vial stitches, ensuring the ileostomy remains flush with the skin.
The surgeon positions the drain through the ileostomy, valve, and into the pouch, securing it with a silk stitch to maintain its placement.
The surgeon completes the operation by performing a colectomy and closing the previous colostomy site, ensuring the surgical area is properly sealed.
